texto: That which attracts the world must please and pander to the self-importance of man. The world itself is a vain show, and likes its own. Consequently there is nothing which so carries the mass of men along with it as that which flatters the vanity of the human mind. It may assume the lowliest air, but sinful man seeks his own honour and present exaltation.
explicacao: Orientação direta de William Kelly sobre temas ligados a reflexao. A afirmação “That which attracts the world must please and pander to the self-importance of man. The world its…” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a reflexao, god, vanity, útil em momentos de vanity e world.

frase_original: That which attracts the world must please and pander to the self-importance of man. The world itself is a vain show, and likes its own. Consequently there is nothing which so carries the mass of men along with it as that which flatters the vanity of the human mind. It may assume the lowliest air, but sinful man seeks his own honour and present exaltation.
